initial_draft:
<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED and I have 1 unit of X. The cost to produce it was 40 ZUP. I need to sell this for a profit. Player BLUE has offered to buy resources using ZUP. Since I have only 1 unit of X, I can only make one trade involving that single unit. I should propose a trade where I give 1 X and ask for a reasonable amount of ZUP. A fair market value would be slightly above the production cost, perhaps 50 ZUP, to ensure a 10 ZUP profit. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I am selling my 1 unit of X. I produced it for 40 ZUP, so I am asking for 50 ZUP for it. Let's make a deal.</message>

iterations.feedback:
<critique>
1. Format: OK. All required tags are present in the correct order, and the content within `<newly proposed trade>` follows the specified format "Player RED Gives X: amount | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount".
2. Payoff alignment: OK. The proposal asks for 50 ZUP for an item costing 40 ZUP, yielding a 10 ZUP profit. Since I have only 1 unit of X, this is the maximum profit I can extract in a single trade. No better split exists for this specific item.
3. Opponent plausibility: OK. The opponent (Player BLUE) is buying resources. Asking for a small premium (25% over cost) is standard for a seller with limited inventory. There is no reason to believe this will be rejected immediately unless BLUE has a strict budget cap not yet revealed, but it is a reasonable starting point.
4. Consistency: OK. The reasoning explains the cost basis and the desired profit margin, which aligns perfectly with the 50 ZUP ask. The message is a polite invitation to trade, consistent with a PROPOSAL action.
5. Rule compliance: OK. This is the first proposal (count 1), well within the limit of 3 proposals.
</critique>
